perturbation.original_text: Confidential information means any information disclosed to by one party to the other, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally or by inspection of tangible or intangible objects, including without limitation documents, business plans, source code, software, documentation, financial analysis, marketing plans, customer names, customer list, customer data.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information means any information disclosed to by one party to the other, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally or by inspection of tangible or intangible objects, including without limitation documents, business plans, source code, software, documentation, financial analysis, marketing plans, customer names, customer list, customer data, except any information related to trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'confidential information', the contract creates ambiguity and potential conflict with laws protecting trade secrets. In this NDA's original language, both parties agree to not disclose trade secrets. With this change, one party may disclose trade secrets without consequence, as they are no longer considered 'confidential information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law provides broad protection for trade secrets. By explicitly excluding trade secrets from the definition of confidential information, the contract weakens protection afforded by MA law and creates a legal contradiction. Under Chapter 93 section 42, Trade Secret misappropriation is defined and covered, while this agreement would now allow that misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'confidential information', directly contradicts the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under Massachusetts law, specifically Chapter 93, Section 42, as indicated in both scraped snippets. The law defines 'misappropriation' to include unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets. By removing trade secrets from the definition of confidential information, the contract effectively allows for the misappropriation (disclosure or use) of trade secrets without consequence, weakening the legal safeguards the law aims to provide.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unau- thorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Receiving Party shall take at least those measures that Receiving Party takes to protect its own most highly confidential information and shall have its employees, if any, who have access to Confidential Information sign a nonuse and non-disclosure agreement in content substantially similar to the provi-sions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take measures it deems appropriate to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Receiving Party shall take measures that Receiving Party finds most suitable to protect its own most highly confidential information and may have its employees, if any, who have access to Confidential Information sign a nonuse and non-disclosure agreement in content substantially similar to the provi-sions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable measures' to 'measures it deems appropriate' and 'shall' to 'may' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This introduces subjectivity and reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Massachusetts version, MGL c. 93 Section 42)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the standard of care required to protect trade secrets under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By allowing the Receiving Party to determine the appropriate level of protection, it contradicts the reasonable measures standard implied by the act, making it harder to prove misappropriation.

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall take all reasonable measures' to 'shall take measures it deems appropriate' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text imposes a clear, objective standard of 'reasonable measures', while the changed text introduces subjectivity, allowing the Receiving Party to decide what is appropriate. The same weakening occurs with 'shall have' being changed to 'may have'. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality clause. The Law Explanation supports this by stating it undermines the standard of care required under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' to include breach of a 'confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information'. By allowing the Receiving Party to subjectively determine the measures, the contract creates ambiguity and reduces the standard of care which could be argued as a breach of duty. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' where the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall survive for a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall survive for a period of 3 years or until either party deems it no longer necessary, from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'or until either party deems it no longer necessary' creates a contradiction. The original clause provides a fixed term, whereas the modified clause allows for unilateral termination, undermining the original term and creating uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Contract Law - specifically, principles of mutual obligation and definiteness of terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines the mutuality of obligation required for a valid contract. The added clause gives one party the power to unilaterally terminate the agreement at any time, rendering the promise illusory and potentially making the entire agreement unenforceable. A contract requires consideration from both parties, and this removes consideration from the party able to unilaterally cancel.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The added clause introduces a unilateral termination condition, contradicting the original fixed term and undermining the mutuality of obligation, which is essential for a valid contract. The Law Explanation indicates the modification gives one party the power to unilaterally terminate the agreement at any time. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable,' implying that a unilateral termination clause, which effectively removes obligation (consideration) from one party, makes the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement may be adjudicated in any jurisdiction the Disclosing Party chooses.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts' to 'may be adjudicated in any jurisdiction the Disclosing Party chooses,' the contract introduces forum selection uncertainty and potentially violates principles of fairness and reasonableness in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law regarding Forum Selection Clauses (unenforceable if unreasonable or obtained through fraud).
perturbation.law_citation: M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows the Disclosing Party to choose any jurisdiction, potentially including one with no connection to the parties or the dispute. This is unreasonable and could be seen as oppressive to the Receiving Party. In the Bremen case, the Supreme Court ruled that forum selection clauses are enforceable if reasonable, whereas this new clause would allow for any forum.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. | 407 U.S. 1 (1972) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972) Overview Opinions Materials Argued: March 21, 1972 Decided: June 12, 1972 Syllabus U.S. Supreme Court The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972) The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. No. 71-322 Argued March 21, 1972 Decided June 12, 1972 407 U.S. 1 Syllabus Petitioner Unterweser made an agreement to tow respondent's drilling rig from Louisiana to Italy. The contract contained a forum-selection clause providing for the litigation of any dispute in the High Court of Justice in London. When the rig under tow was damaged in a storm, respondent instructed Unterweser to tow the rig to Tampa, the nearest port of refuge. There, respondent brought suit in admiralty against petitioners. Unterweser invoked the forum clause in moving for dismissal for want of jurisdiction and brought suit in the English court, which ruled that it had jurisdiction under the contractual forum provision. The District Court, relying on Carbon Black Export, Inc. v. The Monrosa, 254 F.2d 297, held the forum-selection clause unenforceable, and refused to decline jurisdiction on the basis of forum non conveniens. The Court of Appeals affirmed. Held: The forum-selection clause, which was a vital part of the towing contract, is binding on the parties unless respondent can meet the heavy burden of showing that its enforcement would be unreasonable, unfair, or unjust. Pp. 407 U. S. 8 -20 428 F.2d 888 and 446 F.2d 907, vacated and remanded. BURGER, C. J., delivered the opinion of the Court, in which BRENNAN, STEWART, WHITE, MARSHALL, BLACKMUN, POWELL, and REHNQUIST, JJ., joined. WHITE, J., filed a concurring statement, post, p. 407 U. S. 20 . DOUGLAS, J., filed a dissenting opinion, post, p. 407 U. S. 20 . Page 407 U. S. 2 Read More Opinions Opinions & Dissents U.S. Supreme Court The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972) The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. No. 71-322 Argued March 21, 1972 Decided Ju
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates disputes be adjudicated exclusively in Massachusetts, while the changed text permits the Disclosing Party to choose any jurisdiction. This directly contradicts the principle established in The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. case (as per Scraped Law Snippet 2), which requires forum selection clauses to be 'reasonable, unfair, or unjust' to be unenforceable. Allowing the Disclosing Party to choose any jurisdiction, even one with no connection to the parties or dispute, is likely unreasonable and oppressive to the Receiving Party, creating a strong legal contradiction.
